prompt:
I'm moving my blog off a hosted platform and I want a tiny static site generator I fully understand. Deliverable: `python3 sitegen.py build ` turns a flat directory of .md sources into HTML. It runs in CI and the output gets committed, so builds must be fully deterministic — same sources in, byte-identical files out, every run. Each source starts with front matter between two `---` lines: `title:` and `date: YYYY-MM-DD` are required, optional `draft: true` means skip the file entirely (no page, not in the index). After the front matter, my markdown dialect is deliberately small: `# ` and `## ` headings; `- ` lines form bulleted lists; runs of plain lines separated by blank lines are paragraphs (a paragraph's source lines get joined with single spaces); inline `**bold**`, `*italic*` and `` `code` `` spans. Everything is HTML-escaped — I write about code, so a literal `a < b & c` in a paragraph, or `<` inside a backtick span, must come out as entities, never raw. Each page foo.md becomes foo.html containing `` (the escaped front-matter title) and the converted body, elements in source order. Non-.md files in srcdir are ignored. Also generate an `index.html` with `<title>Index` and a `
    ` of `
  • Title
    • ` links to every page, newest date first, ties broken by title A-Z. Validation is all-or-nothing: if ANY source is missing title or date, or has a malformed date, print an error to stderr that names the offending file and exit 1 WITHOUT writing any output files — I never want a half-built site dir. Create outdir if needed. Acceptance tests are in test_sitegen.py (do not edit); `python3 test_sitegen.py` must pass. Use a few well-separated modules — front-matter parsing, the markdown-lite renderer, and page/index assembly shouldn't share one file.
